title: This 25-Year-Old Built a 2-Year Moat Nobody Can Bet Against | Corgi, Nico Laqua & Emily Yuan
summary: Nico Laqua and Emily Yuan, co-founders of Corgi insurance, secured $108M in Series A funding by rebuilding the insurance industry from first principles rather than modernizing legacy carriers. The founders emphasize pursuing ambitious versions of ideas and building defensible moats through time, energy, and relentless execution despite years of regulatory challenges.
writeup: Corgi co-founders Nico (CEO) and Emily (COO) walk through the founding of an AI-native insurance company targeting tech startups, now tracking toward several hundred million dollars in ARR. The central argument is that the dysfunction in a $100-billion-plus market (roughly 12% of U.S. GDP) is structural: legacy carriers operating by fax and phone call are the underlying problem, not the broker websites layered on top of them. This diagnosis forced a pivot from YC-batch insurance brokerage to becoming a licensed insurance carrier, a multi-year, nearly fatal detour that required raising roughly $80 million pre-revenue.
key_points: Corgi started as an insurance broker at YC, sold tens of thousands in premium and had good early growth, then deliberately shut that down to become a licensed insurance carrier once the founders realized the actual problem was the policy product itself, not the distribution layer.

Legacy carriers running 100- to 200-billion-dollar businesses still route every single policy through fax machines and manual phone calls; that operational reality, not poor marketing, is why insurtech startups built on top of them cannot produce a good product.
Becoming an insurance carrier required raising roughly $80 million almost entirely pre-revenue and took several years, during which Corgi was repeatedly near default-dead and skipped YC demo day.

The founders argue that being outsiders to a regulated industry is an advantage rather than a handicap: incumbents have a calcified mental model of how the stack must work, whereas a blank-slate team can question every assumption and rebuild from first principles using AI.

Nico's framework for startup ideas inverts the conventional risk-reduction instinct: scaling a big idea down to something small does not make it more likely to succeed, it makes it less defensible, because ambition attracts the best talent and investors while difficulty creates a moat that is nearly impossible to replicate.

The infrastructure cost of becoming a licensed carrier is itself a durable barrier: a competitor would need to spend comparable time and capital to replicate the foundation, which is why a Corgi 2.0 is structurally hard to build.
